ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general espresso beverage entry; double espresso (doppio) is a serving-size variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 codes espresso generically; double-shot variant mapped to closest parent coffee espresso code A00LC.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 is the closest parent for coffee beverage; no specific double espresso code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard double espresso (doppio) is approximately 60 ml (60 g), consisting of two 30 ml espresso shots pulled from approximately 14–18 g of finely ground coffee.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed, espresso, restaurant-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14209
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for brewed espresso (restaurant-prepared) used as the nutrient basis per 100 g. A double espresso is two standard shots (~60 ml total) of the same beverage; per-100g values are identical to single espresso. Values scaled from USDA SR Legacy entry 14209 / FDC ID 171890.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of black coffee and espresso — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed consensus
gi_source.source_match_notes: Black coffee and espresso contain negligible digestible carbohydrates (~1.67 g per 100 ml) and have an effectively zero glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature consistently classify plain black coffee as having no meaningful glycemic impact. Per Project Veritas convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because the carbohydrate contribution per 60 g serving is approximately 1.0 g, yielding GL = (1 x 1) / 100 = 0.01, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; moderate caffeine intake
hypertension.guidance: A double espresso contains approximately 8 mg sodium per 60 ml serving, posing negligible sodium load; however, individuals with hypertension should be aware that caffeine can transiently raise blood pressure and should limit intake to 1–2 espresso servings per day as per clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain double espresso without added sugar or milk contains approximately 1.0 g of carbohydrate per serving and has a glycemic load of effectively zero, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without sweeteners or high-fat dairy additions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Minimal saturated fat; antioxidant polyphenols in coffee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A double espresso provides negligible saturated fat (~0.02 g per serving) and delivers chlorogenic acid antioxidants associated with modest cardioprotective effects in epidemiological studies; moderate unsweetened espresso consumption (up to 3–4 cups per day) is not contraindicated in most cardiovascular disease management guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A double espresso, also known as a doppio, is a concentrated coffee beverage consisting of two standard espresso shots (approximately 60 ml total) extracted under high pressure (8–10 bar) from approximately 14–18 g of finely ground, dark-roasted coffee beans. The result is a rich, intensely flavoured beverage with a characteristic reddish-brown crema on top. The flavour profile is bold, slightly bitter, and complex, with notes that vary by roast and origin — ranging from dark chocolate and caramel to fruity or earthy tones. Texturally, espresso is thick and syrupy compared to drip coffee. Nutritionally, a plain double espresso is extremely low in calories (~5 kcal per 60 ml serving), contains negligible carbohydrates, fat, and protein, and has a glycemic load of effectively zero. It is naturally free of added sugars, dairy, and gluten. Espresso is a significant dietary source of caffeine (~120–140 mg per double shot) and provides potassium, riboflavin (vitamin B2), and niacin (vitamin B3). It also contains chlorogenic acids, which are polyphenolic antioxidants studied for potential benefits in glucose metabolism and cardiovascular health. In Singapore and Southeast Asia, double espresso is served in cafes and bistros as a standalone beverage or as the base for lattes, cappuccinos, and flat whites. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets when consumed plain. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or high-fat milk, though caffeine-sensitive individuals and those with hypertension should moderate intake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a double espresso?
Is double espresso good for diabetics?
Double espresso vs single espresso nutrition
Can I drink espresso if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for restaurant-prepared espresso (FDC ID 171890), which is an approved primary source. Double espresso is a serving-size variant of single espresso with identical per-100g composition. GI classification is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database consensus for plain black coffee.
notes: Double espresso (doppio) is two shots of espresso (~60 ml). Per-100g nutrient values are identical to single espresso as the beverage composition does not change with serving size. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — naturally zero as plain espresso contains no added or free sugars beyond trace amounts below reporting threshold. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — naturally zero as espresso is a filtered aqueous extract with no intact dietary fibre. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central reporting. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this entry. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no meaningful glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Project Veritas convention for zero-GI beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per 60 g serving is approximately 1.0 g yielding negligible GL. Cuisine tag 'Malaysian' in the input record reflects the restaurant context (The Rainbow Bistro) rather than the dish origin; espresso is an Italian-origin international beverage and has been reclassified accordingly. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore.
